payload.audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

content_quality.default_object_model: Use Dali::Ui::View rather than Dali::Actor for app-facing object examples.
content_quality.guide_assumption: The reader is using dali-ui to build applications.

example_candidates.lookup_instruction: Open this file only if more concrete usage evidence is needed.

code_verification: receiver method exists on the receiver type
static methods are called statically and member methods are called on valid instances
assignment target is compatible with the method return type
argument count, argument type, and overload shape match the public API
enum owner and enum value both exist
property constants use the correct owner and value type
signal callback signatures match the public signal typedef
code blocks do not rely on inferred namespaces or overloads from naming alone
draft_requirements: Write enough practical detail for application developers to use the feature in a dali-ui app.
Ground natural-language behavior claims in the actual public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when the context pack is not enough.
Before writing examples, inspect the referenced public headers and relevant sample files when needed.
Prefer examples copied or adapted from existing samples when they match the feature; otherwise keep examples small and verify every API use.
After drafting, review the completed Markdown line by line against repository evidence and fix inaccurate explanations or code before returning.
repair_requirements: Repair only the problematic spans and the smallest surrounding context needed; do not rewrite the whole document.
For each listed issue, use the repository evidence and context pack to verify the corrected wording or code before returning.
If an issue reveals the same API misuse nearby, fix the nearby equivalent misuse too; do not make unrelated style rewrites.
After repair, re-check the edited lines against public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when needed.

rules: Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.
Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.
Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples.

rendered_prompt:
You are reviewing an existing DALi dali-ui application developer guide draft.
This is Phase 2.3.5: source-grounded prose review.

Primary goal:
- Improve natural-language accuracy and guide flow using repository evidence.
- Preserve the document's useful structure and examples whenever possible.
- Do not perform a full rewrite.
- Keep the revised user-facing guide in en only.
- Remove Korean, Japanese, Chinese, or other non-English prose from the revised guide.

Feature target:
- Feature: Adaptor Framework (adaptor-framework)
- Category: application-framework
- Guide profile: dali-ui-app
- Audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

Review rules:
- Review every natural-language paragraph line by line against public headers, implementation files, samples, and UTCs when needed.
- Accuracy is the first priority. Flow is the second priority.
- Edit the smallest unit needed: word, sentence, paragraph, or section-local addition/removal.
- If a word-level change is enough, change only the word.
- If a sentence-level change is enough, change only the sentence.
- If an entire paragraph is based on a wrong premise, revise, add, or remove that paragraph.
- Preserve code blocks when possible.
- If prose can be corrected to match an already accurate code block, prefer changing prose.
- If the code block itself makes the prose inaccurate or breaks the guide flow, rewrite only the smallest necessary part of that code block and verify it against public headers/source.
- Do not invent APIs.
- Do not add generic filler just to make the document longer.
- Do not change section structure unless prose_review.allow_section_restructure is true or the current prose is clearly misleading.
- Prefer localized edits when prose_review.localized_edits_only is true.
- Preserve code blocks especially when prose_review.preserve_code_blocks is true.
- Prefer Dali::Ui::View, typed setters, fluent chaining, and view-tree idioms for app-facing guidance.
- Treat dali-core and dali-adaptor APIs as reference/context unless the feature explicitly requires them.

Context summary:
{
  "content_quality": {
    "overview_style": "very_short",
    "require_table_of_contents": true,
    "require_rich_api_examples": true,
    "require_backticks_for_api_names": true,
    "topic_sections_must_be_feature_specific": true,
    "avoid_mechanical_api_listing": true,
    "default_object_model": "Use Dali::Ui::View rather than Dali::Actor for app-facing object examples.",
    "guide_assumption": "The reader is using dali-ui to build applications."
  },
  "prose_review": {
    "enabled": true,
    "apply_to": "all",
    "preserve_code_blocks": true,
    "localized_edits_only": true,
    "allow_section_restructure": false,
    "output_review_report": true,
    "fail_open_on_parse_error": true
  },
  "surface_usage": {
    "primary_surface": "dali-ui",
    "rules": [
      "Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.",
      "Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.",
      "Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples."
    ],
    "symbol_counts": {
      "dali-adaptor": 180
    }
  },

Original Markdown draft:
---
title: Adaptor Framework
sidebar_label: Adaptor Framework
category: application-framework
---

# Adaptor Framework

The adaptor framework starts a DALi application, owns the platform lifecycle, and gives a dali-ui app the point where its `Dali::Ui::View` tree should be created.

## Table of Contents

- [Starting a dali-ui Application](#starting-a-dali-ui-application)
- [Handling Application Lifecycle Signals](#handling-application-lifecycle-signals)
- [Reacting to Device and Locale Changes](#reacting-to-device-and-locale-changes)
- [Rendering Without a Visible Window](#rendering-without-a-visible-window)

## Starting a dali-ui Application

Use `Dali::Application` as the normal entry point for a dali-ui app. Create the `Dali::Application` in `main()`, connect `InitSignal()`, and create your `Dali::Ui::View` hierarchy from that init callback. This keeps DALi object creation aligned with adaptor initialization.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

namespace
{
void CreateUi(Dali::Application application)
{
  auto window = application.GetWindow();

  Dali::Ui::View root =
    Dali::Ui::View::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(Dali::Ui::MATCH_PARENT)
      .SetRequestedHeight(Dali::Ui::MATCH_PARENT)
      .SetBackgroundColor(Dali::Ui::UiColor(0x20242AFF));

  Dali::Ui::View panel =
    Dali::Ui::View::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(360.0f)
      .SetRequestedHeight(240.0f)
      .SetRequestedPositionX(48.0f)
      .SetRequestedPositionY(48.0f)
      .SetBackgroundColor(Dali::Ui::UiColor(0xFFFFFFFF))
      .SetCornerRadius(18.0f);

  root.Add(panel);
  window.Add(root);
}
} // namespace

int DALI_EXPORT_API main(int argc, char** argv)
{
  Dali::Application application = Dali::Application::New(&argc, &argv);
  application.InitSignal().Connect(&CreateUi);
  application.MainLoop();
  return 0;
}
```

`Dali::Application::New()` accepts the process argument list. The adaptor recognizes DALi window and environment options such as width, height, DPI, and help, then updates `argc` and `argv` after consuming them.

## Handling Application Lifecycle Signals

`Dali::Application` exposes lifecycle signals for the common app states. Use `InitSignal()` to build the initial `Dali::Ui::View` tree, `PauseSignal()` and `ResumeSignal()` to stop and resume app activity, and `TerminateSignal()` to release application-owned resources.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

namespace
{
Dali::Ui::View gRoot;

void OnInit(Dali::Application application)
{
  auto window = application.GetWindow();

  gRoot =
    Dali::Ui::View::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(Dali::Ui::MATCH_PARENT)
      .SetRequestedHeight(Dali::Ui::MATCH_PARENT)
      .SetBackgroundColor(Dali::Ui::UiColor(0x101820FF));

  window.Add(gRoot);
}

void OnPause(Dali::Application application)
{
  if(gRoot)
  {
    gRoot.SetOpacity(0.65f);
  }
}

void OnResume(Dali::Application application)
{
  if(gRoot)
  {
    gRoot.SetOpacity(1.0f);
  }
}

void OnTerminate(Dali::Application application)
{
  gRoot.Reset();
}
} // namespace

int DALI_EXPORT_API main(int argc, char** argv)
{
  Dali::Application application = Dali::Application::New(&argc, &argv);

  application.InitSignal().Connect(&OnInit);
  application.PauseSignal().Connect(&OnPause);
  application.ResumeSignal().Connect(&OnResume);
  application.TerminateSignal().Connect(&OnTerminate);

  application.MainLoop();
  return 0;
}
```

Call `Quit()` when the app should leave its main loop. Call `Lower()` when the app should move behind other applications without fully quitting.

```cpp
void RequestClose(Dali::Application application, bool keepWarm)
{
  if(keepWarm)
  {
    application.Lower();
  }
  else
  {
    application.Quit();
  }
}
```

## Reacting to Device and Locale Changes

The adaptor framework forwards device and locale changes through `Dali::Application`. Use `LanguageChangedSignal()` and `RegionChangedSignal()` when text, formatting, or content selection depends on the current locale. Use `LowBatterySignal()`, `LowMemorySignal()`, and `DeviceOrientationChangedSignal()` to adapt app behavior to device state.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

namespace
{
Dali::Ui::View gContent;

void OnInit(Dali::Application application)
{
  auto window = application.GetWindow();

  gContent =
    Dali::Ui::View::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(Dali::Ui::MATCH_PARENT)
      .SetRequestedHeight(Dali::Ui::MATCH_PARENT)
      .SetBackgroundColor(Dali::Ui::UiColor(0xFFFFFFFF));

  window.Add(gContent);
}

void OnLanguageChanged(Dali::Application application)
{
  auto language = application.GetLanguage();
  auto region   = application.GetRegion();

  if(gContent)
  {
    gContent.InvalidateMeasure();
    gContent.InvalidateArrange();
  }
}

void OnLowBattery(Dali::DeviceStatus::Battery::Status status)
{
  if(status == Dali::DeviceStatus::Battery::CRITICALLY_LOW && gContent)
  {
    gContent.SetOpacity(0.8f);
  }
}

void OnLowMemory(Dali::DeviceStatus::Memory::Status status)
{
  if(status == Dali::DeviceStatus::Memory::CRITICALLY_LOW && gContent)
  {
    gContent.RemoveAllChildren();
  }
}

void OnOrientationChanged(Dali::DeviceStatus::Orientation::Status status)
{
  if(gContent)
  {
    gContent.InvalidateArrange();
  }
}
} // namespace

int DALI_EXPORT_API main(int argc, char** argv)
{
  Dali::Application application = Dali::Application::New(&argc, &argv);

  application.InitSignal().Connect(&OnInit);
  application.LanguageChangedSignal().Connect(&OnLanguageChanged);
  application.RegionChangedSignal().Connect(&OnLanguageChanged);
  application.LowBatterySignal().Connect(&OnLowBattery);
  application.LowMemorySignal().Connect(&OnLowMemory);
  application.DeviceOrientationChangedSignal().Connect(&OnOrientationChanged);

  application.MainLoop();
  return 0;
}
```

Use `Dali::Application::GetResourcePath()` for app resource lookup when assets are packaged with the application.

```cpp
void LoadApplicationResources()
{
  auto resourcePath = Dali::Application::GetResourcePath();
}
```

## Rendering Without a Visible Window

Use `Dali::OffscreenApplication` when the app needs DALi rendering without creating a visible native window. This is useful for background rendering, testing, or frame capture workflows. Unlike `Dali::Application`, `Dali::OffscreenApplication` does not run its own main loop; the application controls event processing and rendering.

```cpp
#include <dali/devel-api/adaptor-framework/offscreen-application.h>

int RenderOneOffscreenFrame(int argc, char** argv)
{
  Dali::OffscreenApplication application =
    Dali::OffscreenApplication::New(
      &argc,
      &argv,
      Dali::OffscreenApplication::FrameworkBackend::ECORE,
      Dali::OffscreenApplication::RenderMode::MANUAL);

  application.Start();

  auto offscreenWindow = application.GetWindow();

  application.RenderOnce();
  application.Terminate();

  return 0;
}
```

Choose `Dali::OffscreenApplication::RenderMode::AUTO` when rendering should be driven continuously by the surrounding event loop. Choose `Dali::OffscreenApplication::RenderMode::MANUAL` when the app should render only after an explicit `RenderOnce()` call.
